note_on_ordering: Comparison sequence with candidate-c first (then a) is the required final strongest-parent-versus-candidate ordering; it is flipped from the sibling judge who observed A then C. Ordering does not change the preferred candidate — that emerges from the observed friction differences.

comparisons.objective_pass_acknowledgement: Both candidates pass all seven gates as persisted; harness not rerun.
comparisons.regression_assessment: Neither candidate regresses on any of the seven objective gates. Compared to candidate-c, candidate-b adds an explicit Save-progress control, a Clear-progress destructive-safety dialog (Confirm + Cancel + Escape) and a progressbar landmark. Compared to candidate-b, candidate-c drops those features. No functional regression versus objective gates in either direction.
concrete_observed_rationale: candidate-b exposes a progressbar landmark with valuenow/valuetext that surfaces where the user is in the flow; candidate-c has no progressbar landmark.
candidate-b provides an explicit Save-progress button with a live status announcement; candidate-c autosaves silently on input/change, so a user cannot deliberately verify that state was saved.

candidate-b offers a full Confirm + Cancel + Escape destructive-safety flow on Clear-saved-progress (Cancel default-focused, Escape dismisses, Confirm required); candidate-c offers no user-initiated Clear-progress affordance at all.
candidate-c has fewer controls overall (42 vs 47) which is a small cognitive-load win, but not large enough to offset the missing feedback and destructive-safety affordances.
comparisons.uncertainty: The cognitive-load advantage of candidate-c is real and could be preferred by users who dislike optional chrome; scoring reflects a modest but not overwhelming preference.

comparisons.objective_pass_acknowledgement: Both candidates pass all seven gates as persisted; harness not rerun.
comparisons.regression_assessment: Neither candidate regresses on any of the seven objective gates. Candidate-a additionally provides explicit Save-progress, a full Clear-progress destructive-safety flow, a progressbar landmark, and — uniquely among the three — preserves the confirmation region and keeps Place order disabled across a post-confirmation reload. Candidate-c is functionally lighter but drops those affordances.
concrete_observed_rationale: candidate-a preserves duplicate-safety even across a browser reload: after successful placement, page.reload() still shows the confirmation region and Place order remains disabled (place_order_disabled=true, confirmation_visible=true after reload). candidate-c returns the form to editable state with Place order re-enabled after a reload.
candidate-a provides explicit Save-progress with live status announcement; candidate-c does not.
candidate-a provides a full Confirm + Cancel + Escape destructive-safety flow on Clear-saved-progress; candidate-c offers no user-initiated Clear-progress affordance at all.
candidate-a exposes a progressbar landmark; candidate-c has no progressbar landmark.
candidate-c's leaner UI is a real cognitive-load win but is materially outweighed by the additional interruption-safety, feedback and destructive-safety affordances in candidate-a.
comparisons.uncertainty: None material — the four observed friction gaps for candidate-c all touch on affordances the task brief actively probes.
comparisons.observed_pair_ordering_by_this_judge: candidate-c then candidate-a (flipped from sibling judge who observed A then C); the flip is procedural and does not alter the preferred candidate.

comparisons.objective_pass_acknowledgement: Both candidates pass all seven gates as persisted; harness not rerun.
comparisons.regression_assessment: Neither candidate regresses on any of the seven objective gates. Candidate-a and candidate-b are structurally very similar; the observed difference is that candidate-a preserves the confirmation region and keeps Place order disabled across a post-confirmation reload, while candidate-b returns the form to editable state with Place order re-enabled after reload.
concrete_observed_rationale: candidate-a: after page.reload() following successful placement, reload_state.confirmation_visible=true and reload_state.place_order_disabled=true. candidate-b: reload_state.confirmation_visible=false and reload_state.place_order_disabled=false.
Both share: explicit Save-progress with live status, full Confirm + Cancel + Escape Clear-progress destructive-safety, progressbar landmark, identical navigation and error-recovery patterns.

The differentiator is a stronger cross-reload duplicate-safety guarantee in candidate-a; candidate-b's cross-reload behaviour still keeps payment fields empty but does not preserve the placed-order state.
comparisons.uncertainty: The cross-reload behaviour is a single but decisive difference. Users who deliberately reload after placing an order might find candidate-a's behaviour surprising if they expect a fresh session; a design case could be argued either way, but from a friction/harm-avoidance standpoint the preserved confirmation is safer.

dissent_notes: None to preserve at this step. If a downstream reviewer weighs cognitive-load parsimony above interruption/destructive-safety affordances, the c-vs-b and c-vs-a comparisons could shift toward candidate-c; the b-vs-a comparison would still stand on the cross-reload duplicate-safety evidence.
